publications.title: Efficacy and safety of Y90 radioembolization combined with targeted therapy and immunotherapy in patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma: a dual-center, retrospective study
publications.authors: Wang X, Jiang Q, Chen S, et al.
publications.results: 32 uHCC patients; ORR 71.9% by mRECIST and 50.0% by RECIST 1.1. Median PFS 10.5 months and median OS 17.0 months. AFP >=400 ng/mL independently predicted worse PFS and OS; grade 3-4 TRAEs occurred in 43.8% with no treatment-related deaths.
publications.findings: Real-world data suggest triple-modality TARE plus targeted therapy and immunotherapy may provide encouraging antitumor activity with manageable safety in high-risk uHCC.
publications.notes: Accepted article in press; verify final publication details before external use.

publications.title: Global landscape of yttrium-90 clinical trials: a systematic registry-based analysis
publications.authors: Ni Q, Wang K, Jin W, et al.
publications.results: 373 eligible Y90 trials from ClinicalTrials.gov (2000-2025); 86.9% interventional, 93.1% treatment-focused, 70.1% single-center, 54.2% enrolled <=30 patients, 68.4% North America-based, 11.5% Asia-based, and only 18.2% had published results.
publications.findings: Y90 trial activity has expanded but remains dominated by small, early-phase, single-center studies with uneven geography and limited publication of results.
publications.notes: Registry analysis; useful for research landscape and evidence-gap discussion.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Ni Global landscape of y90 clinical trials a systematic registry based analysis.pdf

publications.title: Meta-analysis of efficacy and safety of Yttrium-90 radioembolization (TARE) in the treatment of advanced hepatocellular carcinoma
publications.authors: Lin H, Tan Q.
publications.results: 40 studies including >10,000 patients; TARE improved OS (pooled HR 0.72, 95% CI 0.63-0.83) and PFS (pooled HR 0.78, 95% CI 0.69-0.89) versus controls; grade >=3 adverse events were lower with TARE (pooled OR 0.74, 95% CI 0.59-0.93).
publications.findings: Meta-analysis supports TARE as an effective and generally tolerable option in advanced HCC, with survival and safety benefits versus conventional/systemic controls.
publications.notes: Systematic review/meta-analysis; check included-control heterogeneity before making strong comparative claims.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Lin Meta analysis of efficacy and safety of Yttrium90 radioembolization in the treatment of advanced HCC.pdf

publications.title: Post-TARE Survival Outcomes in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Influence of Pre-TARE Immunotherapy and Targeted Therapy in a Real-World Cohort
publications.authors: Liu XY, Yu YX, Hong JH, et al.
publications.results: 71 HCC patients; patients receiving TARE as initial major treatment had higher ORR than those with >=2 cycles of immunotherapy plus targeted therapy before TARE (87.5% vs 56.4%, p=0.004) and longer median PFS (17.45 vs 4.86 months; HR 0.327, p=0.023). OS was not significantly different.
publications.findings: Baseline treatment history and inflammatory status appear to influence post-TARE response/PFS; findings do not prove superiority of a specific sequence.
publications.notes: Real-world cohort; sequence interpretation requires caution.

publications.authors: Soydal C, Mesci I, Celebioglu EC, et al.
publications.results: Retrospective CRCLM study of 56 patients and 234 lesions using post-therapy Y90 PET/CT dosimetry. Glass n=30 and resin n=26; dose-response correlation was observed in both groups. Mean OS was not significantly different between glass and resin groups.
publications.findings: Strong support for a dose-response framing in CRCLM: response was linked to absorbed tumor dose, while product comparison should be interpreted through delivered dose and distribution rather than product name alone.

publications.findings: Sequential multimodal management may overcome high lung shunt contraindication and enable curative-intent Y90-SIRT plus resection in selected massive HCC cases.
publications.notes: Case report; details should be checked before use in external materials.

publications.title: High-Dose Radioembolization Limited by Lung Shunt for Hepatocellular Carcinoma Supplied by the Inferior Phrenic Artery
publications.authors: Kim HC, Kim DH, Suh M, Paeng JC, Choi JW
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.results: Single-center retrospective study of 43 HCC patients treated with radioembolization via the inferior phrenic artery from 2017 to 2023. Mean posttreatment tumor absorbed dose was 629 Gy; best responses were CR 44.2%, PR 53.5%, and SD 2.3%. Two-year and five-year OS rates were 83.9% and 70.9%. Toxicity was generally acceptable, with one SIR grade 3 pain event and one SIR grade 1 symptomatic radiation pneumonitis.
publications.findings: High-value IPA paper for the dose discussion: lung-shunt limited high-dose radioembolization via IPA can deliver high tumor absorbed dose with strong local response, but lung shunt and pulmonary toxicity remain key planning constraints.
publications.notes: Added from Inferior phrenic artery ZIP. Review exact eligibility and lung-shunt workflow before external use.

publications.title: Perioperative nursing coordination and complication management in Yttrium-90 radioactive microsphere therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma
publications.authors: Li X, Shang Q.
publications.results: 15 HCC patients; all completed Y90 resin microsphere treatment. Postoperative complication rate was 73.3% (11/15), mainly abdominal pain, fatigue, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and low-grade fever; no severe radiation-induced liver injury or significant myelosuppression occurred.
publications.findings: Structured perioperative nursing, radiation protection, vital-sign/lab monitoring, and tiered complication management may support safe Y90 microsphere therapy workflows.
publications.notes: Nursing workflow paper; center-specific experience with small sample size.

publications.title: Radiation Pneumonitis after Yttrium-90 Radioembolization: A Systematic Review
publications.authors: Kis B, Gyano M.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.results: Systematic review compiling 48 reported radiation pneumonitis cases after Y-90 radioembolization. Mortality was 40%; lung dose was higher in glass-treated cases than resin-treated cases, while hepatopulmonary shunt fraction was not significantly different between groups.
publications.findings: Important cautionary review arguing that fixed lung dose thresholds may not be uniformly applicable across devices and that shortcomings in hepatopulmonary shunt calculation should be addressed.
publications.notes: Added from PVT ZIP / Radiation Pneumonitis folder.

publications.title: Radiation Segmentectomy for Early Hepatocellular Carcinoma is Curative
publications.authors: Salem R, Padia SA, Toskich BB, Callahan JD, Fowers KD, Geller BS, Johnson GE, Kulik L, Patel TC, Lewandowski RJ, Kim E.

publications.findings: Strong positioning reference for curative-intent radiation segmentectomy in carefully selected very early/early HCC, especially non-ablatable tumors or challenging locations near critical structures.

publications.title: Transarterial Radioembolization versus Atezolizumab-Bevacizumab for the Treatment of Hepatocellular Carcinoma with Portal Vein Tumor Thrombosis
publications.authors: Park Y, Cho Y, Kim SU, Kim A, Shin H, Kim HC, et al.
publications.results: Multicenter retrospective study of 213 treatment-naïve HCC patients with PVTT without extrahepatic metastasis treated with TARE (n=125) or atezolizumab/bevacizumab (n=88). Median OS was longer with TARE than atezo/bev (27.5 vs 8.6 months, P<0.01), including after IPTW and PSM adjustment. Benefit was observed in Vp1–3 PVTT but not clearly in Vp4. PFS and ORR were similar; adverse events were less frequent with TARE.
publications.findings: Very useful modern comparator paper for PVTT positioning: in selected patients with preserved liver function and segmental/lobar PVTT, TARE may compare favorably with atezolizumab/bevacizumab, but retrospective selection effects must be handled carefully.
publications.notes: Added from immunotherapy 2 folder inside Inferior phrenic artery ZIP. Article available online in 2025.

publications.authors: Choi JW, Suh M, Choi Y, Lee M, Paeng JC, Kim HC.
publications.results: Single-institution cohort of 48 Child-Pugh A patients with unilobar HCC and portal vein invasion treated with Y-90 glass microspheres. publications.title: Arterial Hypoperfusion as a Negative Predictive Marker for Primary Hepatic Malignancies Treated with Y-90 Glass Microsphere Transarterial Radioembolization
publications.authors: Kalaghchi B, Ince S, Barnes J, et al.
publications.results: Retrospective analysis of 88 patients from 400 Y90 treatments who received prescribed dose ≥150 Gy and had pre- or post-treatment SPECT/CT images. Hypoperfused lesions were seen in 11 patients and hyperperfused lesions in 77 patients. Hypoperfusion was associated with shorter PFS (HR 1.87, p=0.039), higher elsewhere liver failure (HR 3.36, p=0.019), higher distant failure (HR 7.64, p<0.001), and worse OS in inverse probability weighted analysis (p=0.032).
publications.findings: Arterially hypoperfused primary liver tumors may respond less favorably to glass Y90 TARE than hyperperfused tumors. Useful for patient selection, expectation setting, and explaining why tumor perfusion and dose distribution matter beyond prescribed activity.
publications.notes: Added from hypo/hyper tumor ZIP. Review exact cohort definitions before external use.

publications.title: Complete Response to Locoregional Therapy Plus Immunotherapy for Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Chiang CL, Chan KSK, Chiu KWH, et al.
publications.results: Cohort study of 63 unresectable HCC patients treated with locoregional therapy plus immunotherapy in two prospective studies. Twenty-nine patients (46.0%) achieved radiologic complete response. CR patients had better 3-year OS than non-CR patients (75.5% vs 28.1%, P<0.001). Among CR patients, 3-year time-to-progression was 58.7% and 3-year local control was 90.5% after a watch-and-wait strategy and IO discontinuation.
publications.findings: Useful locoregional-plus-IO outcome paper showing that durable complete response is possible in locally advanced HCC, especially in patients with smaller tumor burden and no vascular invasion. This is LRT/IO context rather than a pure Y90 paper.
publications.notes: Added from immunotherapy 2 folder inside Inferior phrenic artery ZIP. Treatment exposure was SBRT followed by anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy, with many patients receiving TACE before SBRT; not specifically TARE.

publications.authors: Lee YB, Nam JY, Cho EJ, Lee JH, Yu SJ, Kim HC, Paeng JC, Yoon JH, Kim YJ
publications.results: Single-arm phase I/IIa SOLID trial. Twenty-four patients received Y90 radioembolization and 23 received durvalumab. Median TTP was 15.2 months, median PFS 6.9 months, median OS was not reached, and the 18-month OS rate was 58.3%. ORR by mRECIST was 83.3%, including CR in 29.2% and PR in 54.2%. Grade 3 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 8.7%; no treatment-related serious adverse events were reported.
publications.findings: Strong combination-therapy signal for Y90 followed by durvalumab in locally advanced unresectable HCC, supporting the biologic and clinical rationale for pairing radioembolization with PD-L1 inhibition while still needing randomized validation.

publications.title: Bilobar Radioembolization Carries the Risk of Radioembolization-Induced Liver Disease in the Treatment of Advanced Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Safety and Efficacy Comparison to Systemic Therapy with Atezolizumab/Bevacizumab
publications.authors: Jeschke M, Ludwig JM, Leyh C, et al.
publications.results: Retrospective real-world cohort of 74 advanced HCC patients involving both liver lobes: bilobar TARE n=33 versus atezolizumab/bevacizumab n=41. OS numerically favored systemic therapy (13.0 vs 7.1 months, p=0.07). Similar disease control was observed, but OS in the TARE group was limited by REILD; baseline liver function predicted REILD in cirrhotic patients.
publications.findings: Important cautionary paper: whole-liver/bilobar TARE can carry meaningful liver-toxicity risk, so patient selection, baseline liver function, and treatment volume matter when comparing TARE with modern systemic therapy.

publications.title: Combining Selective Internal Radiation Therapy with Immunotherapy in Treating Hepatocellular Carcinoma and Hepatic Colorectal Metastases: A Systematic Review
publications.authors: Ramdhani K, Smits MLJ, Lam MGEH, Braat AJAT
publications.results: Systematic review of SIRT plus immunotherapy in HCC and mCRC. Four early-phase patient studies with 87 total patients were included. No increased toxicity was observed versus known monotherapy data. The two mCRC studies showed limited response, while HCC studies reported median OS of 16.5 and 16.2 months, median PFS of 5.6 and 5.7 months, and ORR ranging from 31% to 80%.
publications.findings: Good summary paper for the early evidence base: SIRT plus immunotherapy appears feasible and safe, but clinical benefit is still uncertain and disease-specific, with stronger early signals in HCC than mCRC.

publications.authors: Kaya NA, Tai D, Lim X, et al.
publications.results: Translational analysis of 33 patients from a phase 2 trial of Y90 resin microspheres followed by nivolumab for advanced HCC. Prior trial response rate was 30.6%. Responders more often had higher tumor mutation burden, NCOR1 mutations, interferon-gamma pathway expression, and CXCL9+/CXCR3+ macrophage features; non-responders were enriched for an Asian-specific transcriptomic subtype with chromosome 16 deletions and cell-cycle pathway upregulation.
publications.findings: High-value biomarker paper for Y90 plus nivolumab: response biology may differ from conventional ICI biomarkers, supporting the need for combination-specific translational evidence and patient-selection strategies.

publications.authors: Marinelli B, Cedillo M, Pasik SD, et al.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.results: Single-center retrospective review of 29 HCC patients receiving 41 locoregional treatments during nivolumab therapy, including 20 TACE and 21 Y90 TARE procedures. No nivolumab-related deaths or 30-day mortality after LRT were observed. No grade III/IV adverse events were attributed to nivolumab; five grade III/IV hypoalbuminemia or hyperbilirubinemia events occurred within 3 months after LRT.
publications.findings: Supports feasibility of performing TACE or Y90 radioembolization during nivolumab therapy in selected intermediate and advanced HCC patients, with acceptable safety in this retrospective cohort.

publications.title: Comparison of Radioembolization and Sorafenib for the Treatment of Hepatocellular Carcinoma with Portal Vein Tumor Thrombosis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Safety and Efficacy
publications.authors: Kim PH, Choi SH, Kim JH, Park SH
publications.results: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 17 studies in HCC with PVTT. Pooled OS favored radioembolization over sorafenib at 6 months (76% vs 54%) and 1 year (47% vs 24%); time to progression was also longer. Grade 3/4 adverse events were lower with radioembolization (9% vs 28%).
publications.findings: Useful historical comparator for advanced HCC with PVTT before the current immunotherapy era: radioembolization showed better pooled survival and lower severe toxicity than sorafenib, but evidence came from heterogeneous non-randomized studies.
publications.notes: Added from Immunotherapy ZIP. Although this is sorafenib rather than ICI therapy, it is useful background for systemic-therapy comparison and PVTT positioning.

publications.title: Safety of Combined Yttrium-90 Radioembolization and Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Immunotherapy for Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Zhan C, Ruohoniemi D, Shanbhogue KP, et al.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.results: Single-center retrospective study of 26 HCC patients receiving checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy within 90 days of Y90 radioembolization. No 30-day mortality and no early grade 3/4 hepatobiliary or immunotherapy-related toxicities were reported. Median OS was 17.2 months from first immunotherapy and 16.5 months from first radioembolization.
publications.findings: Early safety signal for Y90 plus checkpoint inhibitor sequencing in HCC: combination appeared feasible with limited treatment-related toxicity, but prospective studies are needed to define efficacy, timing, and patient selection.
publications.notes: Added from Immunotherapy ZIP.
publications.pdfFileName: 2019 Zhan Hickey safety of radiembolization plus Y90.pdf

publications.title: Safety of Transarterial Radioembolization with Yttrium-90 Glass Microspheres Without Cystic Artery Occlusion
publications.authors: Topcuoglu OM, Selcuk NA, Sarikaya B, Toklu T
publications.results: Retrospective study of 34 TARE procedures in 29 patients where the cystic artery was near the treatment zone. Mean gallbladder dose was 96.4 Gy; gallbladder activity was seen on SPECT/CT in 32.3%, with no grade 2 or 3 adverse events detected.
publications.findings: Supports that glass Y90 TARE may be safely performed without cystic artery embolization in selected cases when cystic artery origin is close to the treatment area.
publications.notes: Added from Cystic Artery folder in Glass vs Resin ZIP.

publications.title: Yttrium-90 Radioembolization of the Right Inferior Phrenic Artery in 20 Patients with Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Kim HC, Kim YJ, Paeng JC, Chung JW
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.results: Retrospective series of 20 HCC patients treated with Y90 glass microspheres via the right inferior phrenic artery. Systemic-to-pulmonary shunts were seen in 9 patients and embolized before Y90. No symptomatic radiation pneumonitis or cutaneous complications occurred; best RIPA-fed tumor responses included CR in 4 and PR in 9 patients.
publications.findings: Useful technical safety paper for treating HCC supplied by the right inferior phrenic artery: with C-arm CT assessment and prophylactic embolization of systemic-to-pulmonary shunts, direct glass Y90 infusion via RIPA appeared feasible and generally safe.
publications.notes: Added from Inferior phrenic artery ZIP. Important for extrahepatic collateral supply and lung-shunt risk discussion.

publications.title: Radioembolization with Yttrium-90 Microspheres for Patients with Unresectable Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Bhangoo MS, Karnani DR, Hein PN, et al.
publications.results: Retrospective series of 17 unresectable HCC patients receiving 33 Y90 treatments; 65% received TheraSphere and 35% received SIR-Spheres. Median delivered dose was 100 Gy and median lung shunt fraction was 2.02%.
publications.findings: Small real-world HCC series using both glass and resin microspheres; useful as an early clinical-efficacy reference but not designed to compare products head-to-head.
publications.notes: Added from Glass vs Resin ZIP. Review exact toxicity and response values before external use.
publications.pdfFileName: 2015 Bhangoo Radioembolization with Yttrium-90 microspheres for patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma.pdf

publications.title: Use of Yttrium-90 Glass Microspheres (TheraSphere) for the Treatment of Unresectable Hepatocellular Carcinoma in Patients with Portal Vein Thrombosis
publications.authors: Salem R, Lewandowski R, Roberts C, Goin J, Thurston K, Abouljoud M, Courtney A.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.results: Retrospective clinical study of 15 unresectable HCC patients with portal vein thrombosis treated with 29 Y-90 glass microsphere infusions. No procedural complications occurred; treatment was generally well tolerated, with bilirubin changes often associated with disease progression rather than clear treatment toxicity.
publications.findings: Early foundational TheraSphere PVT paper supporting feasibility and hepatic safety of glass Y-90 in selected HCC patients with compromised portal venous flow and no cavernous transformation.
publications.notes: Added from PVT ZIP. Useful historical safety reference for glass microspheres in PVT.

publications.title: Safety and Efficacy of 90Y Selective Internal Radiation Therapy Using Glass Microspheres in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Southeast Asian Single-Institution Initial Experience
publications.authors: Ng CWQ, Bin Hassan MJ, Goh C, Tong AKT et al.
publications.results:
• Safety and Ef fi cacy of 90 Y Selective Internal Radiation Therapy Using Glass Microspheres in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Southeast Asian Single-Institution Initial Experience Cherie Wei Qi Ng 1, Muhammad Jai bin Hassan 1, Charles Goh 1,2, Aaron Kian Ti Tong 1,2, Wei Ying Tham 1,2, Sue Ping Thang 1,2, Winnie Wing-Chuen Lam 1,2, Han Chung Low 1, Butch M.
• Although 5 patients had deranged liver function tests after the procedure, in only 1 patient did this constitute a grade 1 toxicity (in alkaline phosphatase) by the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events.
• Half the patients showed a reduction in serum a -fetoprotein measure- ments, and 6 of 11 patients demonstrated an objective response (complete or partial) on imaging.
• In this case series, we highlight the initial safety of performing 90 Y SIRT using glass microspheres to deliver high tumor-absorbed doses in our local patient population within a single institution in Southeast Asia.

publications.title: Selective internal radiotherapy and chemosaturation show equivalent survival in metastatic uveal melanoma: a retrospective multicenter study
publications.authors: Koch EAT, Schielein LA, Reitmajer M, et al.
publications.results:
• The estimated median overall survival (OS) for SIRT was 25.1 months (95% confidence interval [CI], 15.3-34.8), for TACE, 11.4 months (95% CI, 7.8-14.9), and for PHP, 24.9 months (95% CI, 13.7-36.0).
• In the multivariate analysis that included LDH and additional systemic therapies, there was no significant difference in OS between SIRT and PHP (HR 1.14, 95% CI 0.67-1.93, p = 0.63), whereas TACE was found to be associated with a substantially reduced in OS compared to PHP (HR 3.25, 95% CI 1.31-8.04, p = 0.01).

• In the multivariate analysis that included LDH and additional systemic therapies, there was no significant difference in OS between SIRT and PHP (HR 1.14, 95% CI 0.67-1.93, p = 0.63), whereas TACE was found to be associated with a substantially reduced in OS compared to PHP (HR 3.25, 95% CI 1.31-8.04, p = 0.01).
publications.abstract: BACKGROUND: Metastatic uveal melanoma (UM) primarily affects the liver, and due to low response rates to systemic therapies, liver-directed therapies (LDT) are commonly used for hepatic tumor control. Direct head-to-head trials comparing these LDTs are still lacking. METHODS: A retrospective multicenter explorative analysis was conducted to evaluate the clinical outcomes of transarterial chemoembolization (TACE), selective internal radiotherapy (SIRT), and percutaneous hepatic perfusion (PHP, chemosaturation). RESULTS: The present analysis was conducted on a cohort of 121 patients who had been treated for metastatic UM with LDT at five different skin cancer centers. A total of n = 15 patients received TACE (12.4%), n = 51 SIRT (42.1%), and n = 55 PHP (45.5%). The estimated median overall survival (OS) for SIRT was 25.1 months (95% confidence interval [CI], 15.3-34.8), for TACE, 11.4 months (95% CI, 7.8-14.9), and for PHP, 24.9 months (95% CI, 13.7-36.0). In the multivariate analysis that included LDH and additional systemic therapies, there was no significant difference in OS between SIRT and PHP (HR 1.14, 95% CI 0.67-1.93, p = 0.63), whereas TACE was found to be associated with a substantially reduced in OS compared to PHP (HR 3.25, 95% CI 1.31-8.04, p = 0.01). Adverse events occurred in 41.6% of patients undergoing SIRT, 64.3% of patients receiving TACE, and 84.6% of patients receiving PHP. CONCLUSION: The findings of this study indicate that SIRT and PHP demonstrate equivalent survival rates in a real-world setting.

publications.abstract: Background Complete pathologic necrosis (CPN) at liver transplant is a positive predictor of freedom from recurrence and overall survival for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Comprehensive authorized user parameters for yttrium 90 radiation segmentectomy CPN rates at explant remain undefined. Purpose To evaluate established outcomes for radiation segmentectomy before liver transplant and determine optimal parameters to achieve CPN among patients with treatment-naive HCC. Materials and Methods This multicenter retrospective study included patients with treatment-naive HCC who underwent glass microsphere radiation segmentectomy before liver transplant from January 2016 to December 2024. Treatment parameters were compared for patients that achieved CPN versus those without CPN using Mann-Whitney U or χ2 tests. Receiver operating characteristic analysis was performed to determine treatment parameter thresholds and subsequently predict CPN. Results A total of 303 patients with 364 tumors were included in the study. Tumors had a median size of 2.4 cm (IQR, 2.0-3.1 cm) and demonstrated a CPN rate of 68% (246 of 364). Compared with tumors without CPN, those with CPN had a higher microsphere activity (median, 1242 Bq vs 1203 Bq; P <.03), microspheres per milliliter (median, 9000 per mL vs 7600 per mL; P =.02), single-compartment dose (median, 544 Gy vs 379 Gy; P <.001), and angiosome-to-tumor volume ratio (median, 25.2 vs 15.6; P =.02). Multivariable logistic regression showed that a microsphere activity of 1087 Bq or greater (P =.03), a dose of 440 Gy or greater (P <.001), and an angiosome-to-tumor volume ratio of 16 or greater (P <.001) were independent predictors of CPN. There was a positive association between the number of optimized parameters and CPN at liver transplant, with a positive predictive value of 74% (198 of 269) with one threshold met and 91% (57 of 63) with all thresholds met (P <.001). Conclusion Among patients with treatment-naive HCC, glass microsphere radiation segmentectomy before liver transplant achieved high rates of CPN, which was associated with optimized parameter thresholds determined for microsphere activity, microspheres per milliliter, dose, and angiosome-to-tumor volume ratio. © RSNA, 2026 Supplemental material is available for this article. See also the editorial by Gordon and Lewandowski in this issue.
